tldr: Council debated temporarily eliminating development charges to boost housing construction, with significant procedural delays and public engagement concerns raised. The decision on development charges was deferred to a special meeting on March 2, 2026, and council acknowledged falling short on public notice procedures.
topics: Temporary elimination of development charges for residential construction
Housing affordability and municipal revenue impacts
Development industry crisis and job losses
Strong Mayor Powers and governance procedures
Council public engagement and agenda notification processes

decisions.item: Waive procedure bylaws to allow three late-registered delegates to speak
Defer all business items (excluding proclamations, recognitions, confidential items, and delegations) to special council meeting
decisions.vote: 4-3 (Kearns, Nielsen, Stolti, Mayor Mead-Ward in favour; Galbraith, Sharman, Bentavanya opposed)

decisions.item: Decision on temporary elimination of development charges
By-law Number 11, 2026 - Confirm proceedings of council meeting of February 17, 2026

delegations.speaker: Michael Collins-Williams, CEO, West End Home Builders Association
delegations.summary: Urged council to approve temporary elimination of development charges, citing industry crisis with only 4 condo units sold in 2025 and construction job losses. Argued the revenue loss estimates are overly pessimistic and that eliminating charges would help make entry-level housing affordable for teachers, firefighters, and nurses.

delegations.speaker: Mike, Home Builders Association
delegations.summary: Requested two-year temporary DC elimination to stimulate residential construction during industry crisis. Argued reduced charges would be passed to consumers, improving affordability, and emphasized need for provincial/federal funding support.

delegations.speaker: Jim Thompson
delegations.summary: Opposed DC elimination, arguing it shifts costs to taxpayers through future property tax increases. Raised concerns that 63 of 79 city policies are overdue for review and called for better council accountability and policy oversight.

delegations.speaker: Shannon Gillies
delegations.summary: Advocated for heritage study of 1419 Ontario Street, then requested the property be removed from the study list in favour of another property in a high-density area at greater demolition risk.

delegations.speaker: Brandt Street property owner
delegations.summary: Resident advocated for heritage protection of their property citing demolition risk from condo development. Suggested expanding heritage character area designation to include multiple adjacent properties for equitable protection.

delegations.speaker: David Barker
delegations.summary: Strongly opposed eliminating development charges, arguing it would subsidize developers' profits and shift costs to property taxpayers. Proposed deferring charges until 75% occupancy and called for a referendum on the ballot.

delegations.speaker: Lynn Crosby
delegations.topic: Development charges, Strong Mayor Powers, and public engagement
delegations.summary: Criticized the rushed decision-making process with inadequate public notice (meetings scheduled before a long weekend with daytime timing). Opposed subsidizing developers and called the use of Strong Mayor Powers disrespectful to council, staff, and citizens.

bylaws.title: Confirm the proceedings of council at the meeting of February 17, 2026

key_issues.procedural_concerns: Council acknowledged multiple public engagement failures: agenda released late Friday after delegation deadline, no confirmation emails sent for online delegation forms, and extended delegation window not publicly announced. Legal Services Commissioner confirmed council can proceed despite bylaw non-compliance.
key_issues.development_charges_debate: CFO provided supplementary financial report showing development charge elimination would cost the city $16-42 million. Council was split on whether temporary elimination would stimulate housing construction or simply reduce municipal revenue. The decision was deferred to allow more time for deliberation.
key_issues.governance_concerns: Councillor Nielsen raised concerns about Strong Mayor Powers bypassing normal committee process, forcing staff to work over weekend. Jim Thompson highlighted that 63 of 79 city policies are overdue for review.
key_issues.recognition: Heather Howell, M.M. Robinson High School teacher, recognized for receiving 2026 Governor General's History Award for Excellence in Teaching for her heritage garden project.

next_meeting: 2026-03-02 (Special council meeting to vote on development charges and address deferred business items)
